name: The Driftwood
description: A slouching timber-frame building at the end of the docks, built from salvaged river barge planks and held together by stubbornness and tar. The sign is a piece of actual driftwood with the name burned in. Captain Meridian Thorne won the place in a card game, lost it in another, won it back in dispute that may have involved a small fire, and now considers it home port. When he's in town — which is most of the time, despite claims of imminent departure — he holds court at the corner table with a bottle of whatever arrived on the last barge. Margot of House Ashenbraid runs the actual operation: the kitchen, the books, the cleaning, the bouncing. She was hired as a temporary cook and has been managing the entire establishment for two years. Meredith of the Scarlet Veil performs on evenings — her music starts after sundown and draws a different crowd than the daytime dock workers. The Driftwood is not Bottoms Up. Bottoms Up is where you drink with your neighbors. The Driftwood is where you drink with people who arrived on a boat and might leave on one.

tags: smells of tar and cheap ale
sounds: Meridian telling a story that gets more elaborate with each retelling
Margot shouting orders at the kitchen boy — there is no kitchen boy, she shouts anyway
Dockworkers arguing about whose turn it is to buy
Meredith's lute after sundown — haunting, minor key, centuries of practice
River sounds through the walls — the building is basically on the water
The occasional crash from the kitchen
smells: Tar and damp wood — the building sweats river
Cheap ale and cheaper wine — Meridian's sourcing is unreliable
Margot's cooking — unexpectedly good, she was trained at a temple kitchen
Fish — the docks are right there
atmosphere.lighting: Oil lamps, unevenly distributed. The corner table always has a candle because Meridian insists on ambiance. The stage area has proper lamps for Meredith's performances. The back booths are deliberately dark.

layout.name: The Taproom
layout.description: Long and narrow, following the building's barge-hull shape. A bar made from a ship's gangplank. Mismatched stools. Three long tables and Meridian's corner booth, which he claims by leaving his hat on it even when absent.

layout.name: The Stage Corner
layout.description: A slightly raised platform in the front corner with a stool, lamp hooks, and a sign reading 'PERFORMERS WELCOME — MANAGEMENT NOT RESPONSIBLE.' Meredith performs here most evenings. Other musicians are technically allowed but rarely volunteer after hearing her play.

layout.name: The Kitchen
layout.description: Tiny, efficient, and somehow producing food that's better than the establishment deserves. Margot runs it with temple-trained precision and the quiet fury of someone who expected more from life.

layout.name: The Back Deck
layout.description: A wooden platform extending over the water. Four tables. Popular in summer. In winter, only Meridian sits out here, claiming the cold helps him think. It does not.

layout.name: The Upstairs Rooms
layout.description: Three rooms available for rent by the night. Basic: a bed, a hook, a bucket. Margot keeps them cleaner than the prices warrant.

hooks: Meridian's enchanted compass has been pointing at the same spot upriver for a week — he's pretending not to notice
Meredith's music caused a patron to weep uncontrollably last night — the song was supposed to be cheerful
A sealed crate washed up against the back deck that Margot won't let anyone open until she finishes reading the markings
Kael Stormrunner and Meridian haven't spoken in three days, which is unusual — the smuggler and the captain usually drink together nightly
Someone has been leaving coins under the floorboards — Margot found seven stacks, each exactly thirteen coins

worldContext.location: The Driftwood, river docks (south end of wharf), Millhaven
